stories.title: Marcus & Millichap Arranges $14M Sale, Financing of Illinois LA Fitness-Anchored Retail Property
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap announced the sale of a LA Fitness-anchored, four-suite retail property in Mundelein, Illinois. The property sold for $13.976 millkion. Marcus & Millichap Capital Corporation, a subsidiary of Marcus & Millichap, arranged $9.78 million in acquisition financing.“The Sharko | Weisenbeck | Mendoza Group was the second broker to list this deal, speaking volumes for our proprietary database and Marcus’ platform,” said Austin Weisenbeck, senior managing director. Weisenbeck and Sean Sharko, investment specialists in Marcus & Millichap’s Chicago Oak Brook office, had the exclusive listing to market the property on behalf of the seller, an experienced real estate investor/developer in the Chicagoland area. The Sharko | Weisenbeck | Mendoza Group also procured the out-of-state buyer who was in a 1031 exchange. Dean Giannakopoulos, senior managing director, also based in the firm’s Chicago Oak Brook office, secured the financing.The 89,357-square-foot shopping center is situated on 7.48 acres at 1555 S. Lake St. Built in 2019, the property is anchored by LA Fitness, with Kids Empire, The Dog Stop and The Picklr Club among its other tenants. The post Marcus & Millichap Arranges $14M Sale, Financing of Illinois LA Fitness-Anchored Retail Property appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Marcus & Millichap sells LA Fitness retail in Mundelein, IL for $14M with $9.78M financing arranged
Retail property transaction signals continued investment in stabilized, single-tenant suburban assets
No direct multifamily relevance; retail financing and deal flow data point only
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap Capital arranged a $13.976 million sale and $9.78 million acquisition financing for a four-suite LA Fitness-anchored retail property in Mundelein, Illinois. The transaction reflects ongoing capital availability in suburban retail despite broader sector headwinds. This is a retail-focused deal with minimal implications for apartment development.
stories.impact: Minimal impact to multifamily development. Demonstrates continued CRE lending appetite and stability in Chicagoland market, but retail fundamentals and residential demand signals diverge materially.

stories.title: Colliers Facilitates Sale of 954K-SF Industrial Portfolio in Indianapolis
stories.excerpt: Colliers announced the sale of a six-building industrial portfolio totaling 954,072 square feet located throughout the Northwest, Northeast and Southwest submarkets of the Indianapolis metropolitan area. The portfolio includes properties at 7555 Woodland Drive, 7645 Woodland Drive, 5350 Lakeview Parkway and 7601 Winton Drive in Indianapolis, as well as 12001 Exit 5 Parkway in Fishers and 1380 Perry Road in Plainfield, Indiana. Colliers Industrial Capital Markets advisors Alex Cantu, Alex Davenport, Jeff Devine, Steve Disse and Tyler Ziebel represented seller EQT in partnership with local market advisors Jason Speckman, Jimmy Cohoat and Andrea Hopper. The portfolio was acquired by MLG Capital, based in Brookfield, Wisconsin. “Indianapolis continues to benefit from its position along major Midwestern transportation corridors and as a Midwestern growth market. The portfolio locations in Park 100, Fishers and Plainfield are some of the highest demand user markets in the city,” said Cantu. “We’re seeing strong interest in Midwestern markets, particularly Indianapolis, as investors continue to recognize the region’s economic momentum, strategic location, and long-term growth potential.” The post Colliers Facilitates Sale of 954K-SF Industrial Portfolio in Indianapolis appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Colliers sells 954K SF six-building industrial portfolio across Indianapolis submarkets to MLG Capital
EQT/investor exit; Park 100, Fishers, Plainfield submarkets cited as highest-demand user markets
Strong Midwestern industrial fundamentals signal sustained logistics/supply-chain investment and job growth
stories.summary: Colliers facilitated the sale of a 954,072-square-foot industrial portfolio across six buildings in Indianapolis (Park 100, Fishers, Plainfield) to Wisconsin-based MLG Capital. The sale reflects investor confidence in the Indianapolis market's position along major transportation corridors and its status as a Midwestern growth hub. Market observers cite strong demand and long-term economic momentum in the metro.
stories.impact: Signals robust logistics/supply-chain job growth and in-migration potential in Indianapolis metro. Industrial strength often precedes population and rental-demand growth; multifamily developers should prioritize acquisitions and land in Fishers, Park 100, and northwest Indianapolis submarkets ahead of absorption acceleration.

stories.title: Stand-Alone Data Centers Banned in Tacoma
stories.excerpt: A new interpretation of Tacoma’s zoning code clarifies that stand-alone data centers cannot be permitted under current regulations. In a joint statement released this week, Tacoma City Councilmember Latasha Palmer and Olgy Diaz said local communities have raised concerns about the rapid development of stand-alone data centers, citing potential impacts and increased utility costs. “Many communities across the country are expressing concern about the development of new independent data centers – and some are outright rejecting them – due to the real and potential impacts on amenities, local infrastructure, neighborhood noise levels, jobs, and many other issues,” said the council members in a statement. “We hear these same concerns echoed by Tacoma residents as well.” In light of these concerns, the city’s planning and development services department issued a new interpretation pursuant to the Tacoma Municipal Code, clarifying that single data centers are not permitted under Tacoma’s existing zoning code. The post Stand-Alone Data Centers Banned in Tacoma appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Tacoma zoning code clarified to ban stand-alone data centers; residents cite utility costs and infrastructure strain
Regulatory barrier shifts data-center development pressure to adjacent markets; Portland, Seattle, suburban WA at risk
Removes competing land-use demand; multifamily developers face less industrial competition but demand clarity on Puget Sound alternatives
stories.summary: Tacoma's city council and planning department issued a zoning interpretation banning stand-alone data centers, citing resident concerns over utility costs, infrastructure strain, noise, and job mismatch. Council members cited national trends of data-center rejection and community pushback. The ban effectively forecloses a major alternative land use in the market.
stories.impact: Removes competing land-use competition in Tacoma, preserving ground for multifamily or mixed-use. However, displaced data-center demand will migrate to Seattle, suburban Snohomish County, or Portland—accelerating land costs and infrastructure pressure in those adjacent markets. Multifamily developers in Puget Sound should monitor whether Tacoma's precedent spreads; if it does, demand for residential sites in Seattle/Eastside will intensify, raising acquisition costs and timeline risk.

stories.title: JLL Completes HQ Lease for Sandbox at Richmond’s Davenport Building
stories.excerpt: JLL completed a 6,576-square-foot lease in the Shockoe Slip district of Richmond, Virginia. Sandbox, a Richmond-based consulting firm and provider of fractional finance and accounting, HR, business operations and recruiting services, will occupy the ground-floor space at 1401 E. Cary Street as its new headquarters this month. Originally constructed in 1870, 1401 E. Cary Street occupies a prominent corner at Cary and South 14th streets in Richmond’s historic Shockoe Slip district. JLL’s Kaylin Farruggio and Charlie Polk represented Sandbox in the transaction. Farruggio and Gareth Jones, in partnership with John Jay Schwartz of Have Site Will Travel LTD, represented SNP Properties. JLL’s Katie Chalmers, vice president, PDS, assisted with the buildout; RCS Commercial Moving & Warehousing handled moving logistics for Sandbox. “Companies continue to prioritize workplaces that support culture, collaboration and employee experience,” said Gareth Jones, senior managing director, JLL. “1401 E. Cary Street offered Sandbox a rare opportunity to secure a highly visible headquarters location with authentic historic character and a turnkey creative office environment.” The post JLL Completes HQ Lease for Sandbox at Richmond’s Davenport Building appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Sandbox consulting firm leases 6,576 SF ground floor in Richmond's Shockoe Slip for HQ; emphasizes culture and collaboration
Historic office leasing to emerging services firm signals downtown revitalization and tenant demand for amenity-rich, culturally authentic space
Indicates Richmond's downtown office market absorbing tech-enabled services tenants; nearby residential demand upside
stories.summary: Sandbox, a Richmond-based fractional finance/HR/recruiting services firm, leased 6,576 square feet of ground-floor space at 1401 E. Cary Street in Shockoe Slip as its new headquarters. The transaction underscores tenant prioritization of workplace culture, collaboration, and authentic historic character. The property is a prominently located historic structure that offers turnkey creative office space.
stories.impact: Shockoe Slip office leasing activity suggests downtown Richmond revitalization and growing demand for service-economy jobs (finance, HR, recruiting), which typically employ young, educated workers with strong rental demand. Multifamily developers should position mixed-use or nearby residential projects to capture this inbound talent; Shockoe Slip and surrounding Fan District submarkets are prime sites for Class A apartments targeting white-collar young professionals.

stories.title: Aspen Hospitality Plans Rockefeller Center’s First and Only Hotel
stories.excerpt: Aspen Hospitality, a division of Aspen One, said it will build the first and only hotel in Rockefeller Center as part of its launch of Nell Hotels, a new luxury brand. The Nell New York is set to open in fall 2027 and will be New York City’s only Relais & Chateaux property, Representing an investment of more than $350 million, the Nell New York will be built within the existing 10 Rockefeller Plaza. The 134-key hotel will repurpose former space. “We are honored to welcome The Nell New York to Rockefeller Center and to partner with a brand that shares our commitment to creating meaningful experiences through art, dining, culture, and community,” said EB Kelly, head of Rockefeller Center and senior managing director, Tishman Speyer. “Built nearly a century ago as a city within a city, Rockefeller Center has long offered a singular destination experience. With the arrival of The Nell New York, that experience is now complete.” The post Aspen Hospitality Plans Rockefeller Center’s First and Only Hotel appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Aspen Hospitality launches Nell Hotels luxury brand; first Rockefeller Center hotel; 134 keys; $350M+ investment; fall 2027 opening
Mixed-use/amenity expansion in Manhattan; no apartment impact but signals ultra-premium real estate development confidence
Hospitality capital influx and ultra-dense mixed-use buildout; no direct multifamily relevance
stories.summary: Aspen Hospitality announced a 134-key luxury hotel (The Nell New York) at Rockefeller Center, the first hotel in the 88-year-old complex. Built within 10 Rockefeller Plaza, the $350+ million project leverages existing infrastructure and will become NYC's only Relais & Chateaux property, scheduled to open fall 2027. The development repurposes former office/retail space.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily. However, ultra-premium hospitality investment in Manhattan and mixed-use densification at iconic addresses reinforce that prime NYC real estate remains highly valued and capital-abundant; development feasibility window remains open for well-capitalized multifamily sponsors in secondary Manhattan markets and outer boroughs.

stories.title: There are no offices being built in Philadelphia
stories.excerpt: The drip of office developments springing up in Philadelphia has stilled, at least for now. Following the completion of a 438,000-square-foot building at 2000 Arch Street in the second quarter, there are no office projects in the city’s active pipeline, Bisnow reported. Chubb’s built-to-suit development was the only one still being developed, according to JLL. The lack of projects going up in the City of Brotherly Love stems in part from a dearth of tenants willing to anchor a space years before it’s actually available. “That’s been a really big hurdle when it comes to attracting out-of-market tenants,” JLL Philadelphia […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Philadelphia office pipeline at zero; no projects under construction after 2Q delivery of 438K SF Chubb built-to-suit
Lack of pre-leasing anchor tenants blocks speculative office development; sublease inventory down 1M SF YoY; overall inventory fell 2M+ SF
Weak office demand but inventory declining signals limited competition for alternative uses (residential, mixed-use); zoning/conversion opportunity
stories.summary: Philadelphia's active office development pipeline has emptied following the Q2 completion of a 438,000-square-foot building at 2000 Arch Street (Chubb's built-to-suit). No speculative office projects are under construction; the dearth of pre-leasing anchors remains a major hurdle. Sublease inventory fell 1 million SF year-over-year; overall inventory declined over 2 million SF. Suburban office leasing dropped 43% year-over-year to 436,000 SF in Q2—the slowest quarter on record for that submarket.
stories.impact: Zero office development pipeline is a powerful signal: land and existing office buildings may be available for adaptive reuse (office-to-residential conversion) or repositioning to mixed-use, especially in Center City. Multifamily developers should scout underutilized office inventory in CBD and suburbs for conversion; reduced new supply competition and declining sublease space suggest tight future office supply, which could support conversion economics if rents stabilize. Philadelphia's office malaise creates residential opportunity.

stories.title: In Likely a Culver City Record, Black Equities Buys Multifamily Asset for $106M
stories.excerpt: Culver City, CA – February 12: An aerial of the Kirk Douglas Theatre on in Culver City. photo: Kayla Bartkowski / Los Angeles Times Stanley Black’s Beverly Hills-based investment firm is closing on a nine-figure mixed-use acquisition in another small city a few miles away. Black Equities has paid $106 million for a 115-unit property in what is said to be perhaps the largest multifamily deal in the history of Culver City, Calif., which is one of Los Angeles County’s priciest rental markets. Greystar sold the decade-old property called Access Culver City — which includes 31,000 square feet of fully leased ground-floor retail — at 8770 Washington Boulevard. Approximately $81 million of the purchase price was allocated to the residential component and $25 million to the retail space, according to Sandy Albert, managing partner at investment and development firm CityPads. The deal is roughly equal to an incredible $704,000 per residential unit and $805 per square foot for the retail component. The acquisition comes as Culver City has become one of Southern California’s hottest multifamily investment markets, fueled by media and technology employers and pro-development housing policies. The city has more than 4,500 housing units in the pipeline and has emerged as one of the few municipalities in L.A. County attracting multifamily investment. Newmark represented Greystar on the sale. Neither the buyer nor the seller immediately returned requests for comment. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Black Equities pays $106M for 115-unit Access Culver City; $704K per unit; record deal for small city; includes 31K SF retail
Ultra-high per-unit valuation signals peak pricing in LA-adjacent market; retail component ($805/SF) also elevated
Culver City rents/values saturated; development window likely closed; land/acquisition strategy should pivot to secondary/tertiary LA submarkets
stories.summary: Black Equities acquired Access Culver City, a 115-unit multifamily property, for $106 million ($704,000 per unit), believed to be the largest multifamily deal in Culver City history. The property, a decade-old asset, includes 31,000 SF of fully leased ground-floor retail (valued at $25M, or $805/SF). The residential component was valued at $81M. Culver City is among LA County's priciest rental markets.
stories.impact: Culver City multifamily valuations have reached peak levels ($704K/unit); this suggests limited new-development IRR opportunity in the submarket. Multifamily developers should deprioritize Culver City for new construction; instead, focus on secondary Westside and San Fernando Valley submarkets (Koreatown, Los Feliz, Burbank, Glendale) where valuations remain sub-$600K/unit and land availability higher. Retail component premium ($805/SF) also suggests ground-floor retail leasing is resilient, supporting mixed-use development feasibility.

stories.title: Palm Beach County Pauses Data Center Applications
stories.excerpt: South Florida has entered the data center debate. In one of the first moves to regulate the construction of the asset class in the region, the Palm Beach County Commission paused data center proposals this week until it adopts appropriate zoning, following pushback from residents. Commissioners this week voted 5-2 to freeze new data center applications targeting unincorporated parts of Palm Beach County under a zoning-in-progress doctrine, which allows local governments to suspend proposals while they evaluate changes to zoning regulations. In another vote, commissioners directed county staff to draft a moratorium on new data center proposals. They are expected to finalize the ordinance next month, after which the moratorium would remain in effect for up to a year, or until new zoning regulations are adopted. “This temporary moratorium will allow sufficient time to evaluate the potential impact of these facilities,” said Palm Beach County Mayor Sara Baxter, who initiated the moratorium. “Palm Beach County must ensure that development at this scale is considered with full transparency and a clear understanding of long-term impacts on infrastructure, resources and residents.” The suspension came as concerns grew over one application, which is scheduled to go before the commission next week. Despite the votes, the development, called Central Park Commerce Center, will be allowed to undergo a review since it was filed before the moratorium. The development, also known as Project Tango, called for the construction of a 3.7 million-square-foot data center across 202 acres at 20125 State Road 80/Southern Boulevard in Loxahatchee, facing Route 98, near the Arden residential development and Saddle View Elementary School. It’s a joint venture involving Atlanta-based developer TPA Group, infrastructure contractor Phillips, the Milstein family’s New York-based Ogden Cap Properties and Loxahatchee Groves-based Palm Beach Aggregates. Despite the rapid rise of artificial intellige
bullets: Palm Beach County Commission votes 5-2 to pause data center applications; moratorium on new proposals expected to finalize next month
Temporary freeze under zoning-in-progress doctrine; moratorium may last up to one year until new zoning adopted
Removes competing land-use demand in south Florida; multifamily developers face clearer site inventory but data-center-driven demand migration risk
stories.summary: Palm Beach County Commission voted 5-2 to pause data center applications in unincorporated areas pending zoning review. County staff is drafting a formal moratorium expected to be finalized next month, which could remain in effect for up to one year while new zoning regulations are adopted. The moratorium follows community pushback over infrastructure, utility, and impact concerns.
stories.impact: Removes short-term data-center development competition in Palm Beach County, preserving land for alternative uses (residential, mixed-use). However, frozen data-center demand may redirect to Broward, Miami-Dade, or Miami Beach, intensifying industrial and utility-intensive infrastructure pressure in adjacent markets. Multifamily developers in Palm Beach should benefit from reduced competing land-use bids; monitor whether moratorium extends or narrows zoning after one year. Expect data-center overflow demand in Broward to raise land costs and infrastructure fees.

stories.title: SF approves another condo project for fast-densifying Market Street corner
stories.excerpt: San Francisco officials gave final approval to a condominium project planned for a long-entitled Market Street development site.  Property owner Keller Grover secured the city’s approval for a 23-story, 201-unit condominium tower on the site of a historic Mission Revival building at 1965 Market Street, the San Francisco Chronicle reported. The entitled property is now expected to be listed for sale, with brokers betting it could establish a new benchmark for development sites approved under California’s expanded housing laws. “It will be a barometer of what is going on because it’s the first project going on the market that was […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: San Francisco approves 23-story, 201-unit condo tower at 1965 Market Street; evolved from 96-unit proposal; includes 28 affordable units
Approved under CA state density bonus laws and SB 423 (streamlined approval); first approved project under new state laws to hit market
Will set land-value benchmark for SF development sites; conversion of historic building; expected to be sold soon
stories.summary: San Francisco officials approved a 23-story, 201-unit condominium tower at 1965 Market Street, a decade-long entitlement that grew from a 96-unit initial proposal. The project leverages California's expanded density bonus measures and SB 423 streamlining. It includes 28 affordable units (28% of total) reserved for households under 50% AMI. The approved site is expected to be listed for sale and is projected to set a new benchmark for development-site valuations under the new state laws. The historic mortuary building will be preserved and repurposed as the building's lobby and amenities.
stories.impact: Approval of a large, mixed-income condo tower on Market Street signals that San Francisco's entitlement environment has shifted favorably toward density and streamlined approvals under state law. The forthcoming sale of the development site will establish a critical pricing comp for other SB 423–approved sites across SF and California; multifamily developers should monitor the sale price to calibrate pro formas for other Bay Area projects. Successful conversion and preservation precedent may accelerate adaptive-reuse feasibility in SF's historic office/retail corridors.

stories.title: Related Ross appointment fires up West Palm Beach development debate
stories.excerpt: West Palm Beach residents are pushing back after the city commission appointed a Related Ross executive to the Downtown Development Authority. The appointment of Jordan Rathlev, an executive vice president at Related Ross, added fuel to an ongoing debate over downtown development, as residents demand greater transparency and a larger role in shaping the city’s future, WPTV reported.  In two public meetings this week, residents in “Save West Palm Beach” shirts expressed their concern about rapid development and high-rise construction, arguing the city is moving too quickly and prioritizing developer alignment over community interests.  They criticized what they see as […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Related Ross executive Jordan Rathlev appointed to West Palm Beach Downtown Development Authority; residents push back
Steve Ross investing estimated $10B in WPB: 6M SF office, 1.4M SF condos; DDA scrutiny and resident backlash
City paused downtown master plan due to resident demands; developer influence high but political risk increasing
stories.summary: West Palm Beach's city commission appointed Related Ross EVP Jordan Rathlev to the seven-member Downtown Development Authority, sparking resident backlash over developer alignment and rapid high-rise growth. Residents, wearing "Save West Palm Beach" shirts, demanded greater transparency and community input. Steve Ross's Related company is investing an estimated $10 billion across WPB, including 6 million SF of office and 1.4 million SF of condos. In response, the city paused its downtown master plan to gather resident feedback. Rathlev is the second Related Ross executive on the DDA board.
stories.impact: Political risk escalating in West Palm Beach despite developer capital momentum. Related's outsized influence and 1.4M SF of planned condo development face growing resident opposition; approval timelines for new projects may extend, and design/density concessions may become necessary to secure community support. Multifamily developers considering WPB should factor in extended entitlement risk and potential density caps; window for high-rise approval may narrow if resident coalition gains formal power in plan revision.

stories.title: Douglaston Realty Offloads 7 Cornelia Street in West Village for $25M
stories.excerpt: A business partner involved with the owner of the SoJo Spa Club in New Jersey signed mortgage documents tied to the sale of a West Village building, according to property records. While it’s unclear if Eun Rae Jo is involved in the $25 million purchase of 7 Cornelia Street, the involvement of Hyun Jun An, an investor and partner working alongside Jo, on the financing side raises the question of whether the famed spa owner has acquired yet another Manhattan property. The buyer in the deed transfer filed to property records on Thursday afternoon was masked behind a shell corporation lacking any indication of the beneficial owner. An was the authorized signatory on the $17.6 million mortgage documents from Woori America bank, according to property records. Douglaston Realty Management was the seller, alongside other entities that had a stake in the 2013 acquisition of the property for $24.6 million. Douglaston did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Neither Jo nor An could be reached for comment. Both have been working together on deals recently, including the acquisition of 893 Broadway in the Flatiron District for $31 million in early June and 16 East 18th Street from Revere Capital for $16.3 million the week prior. 7 Cornelia Street sits just south of West Fourth Street, about a block and a half west of Washington Square Park. The five-story prewar building has 47 apartments and two retail slots on the ground floor, according to StreetEasy. Worker-owned cocktail bar Donna occupies one of the two ground-floor spaces. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Douglaston Realty sells West Village building at 7 Cornelia Street for $25M; unclear beneficial owner; SoJo Spa Club investor involvement
Property records show mortgage documents from Woori America; shell company masking buyer identity
No demand signal or development impact; title/investor structuring story with no multifamily relevance
stories.summary: Douglaston Realty sold a West Village building at 7 Cornelia Street for $25 million. The buyer used a shell corporation to mask beneficial ownership, and mortgage documents were signed by Hyun Jun An, an investor and partner of Eun Rae Jo (owner of the SoJo Spa Club in New Jersey). An secured $17.6 million in financing from Woori America bank. Douglaston had purchased the property in 2013 for $24.6 million. Neither Jo nor An could be reached for comment.
stories.impact: Minimal impact. This is a small-cap, opaque transaction in West Village; no development signal or market demand indicator. Transaction is notable primarily for buyer opacity and potential spa-club operator diversification into NYC real estate—not a development or market-demand story.

stories.title: Siemens Financial, First Horizon Lend $65M on Palm Beach Gardens Medical Complex
stories.excerpt: Siemens Financial and First Horizon have supplied $64.8 million of construction financing to develop a hospital and medical outpatient complex in Florida’s Palm Beach County, Commercial Observer has learned. Sina Companies secured the loan for the planned Health Park at Avenir project in Palm Beach Gardens, Fla., that is fully leased to Jupiter Medical Center. The Real Deal reported news of the loan on Thursday but did not identify the lender. Aztec Group negotiated the debt with a team led by Ezra Katz and Sean Harrington. Located at 12001 Northlake Boulevard, Health Park at Avenir will comprise a two-story neighborhood hospital and three-story medical outpatient building spanning 101,517 square feet. The project, which will be built within the 4,752-acre Avenir in Palm Beach Gardens master-planned community, includes an approved future phase for the development of 24,000 square feet of additional medical outpatient space, according to Sina. “We are excited to welcome Jupiter Medical Center to Avenir and further expand access to high-quality health care in Palm Beach Gardens,” Malcolm Sina, executive chairman of Sina Companies, said in a statement. Siemens Financial and First Horizon did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Siemens Financial and First Horizon provide $64.8M construction financing for Health Park at Avenir, Palm Beach Gardens
Hospital and medical-outpatient complex fully leased to Jupiter Medical Center; 101.5K SF plus future 24K SF phase
Healthcare infrastructure investment in master-planned community; signals capital availability for specialized mixed-use
stories.summary: Siemens Financial and First Horizon provided $64.8 million in construction financing for Health Park at Avenir, a hospital and medical-outpatient complex in Palm Beach Gardens developed by Sina Companies. The project comprises a two-story neighborhood hospital and three-story medical outpatient building (101,517 SF) fully leased to Jupiter Medical Center, with an approved future phase for 24,000 SF of additional medical space. The project is located within Avenir, a 4,752-acre master-planned community.
stories.impact: Healthcare-anchored mixed-use development in a large master-planned community signals capital confidence in Avenir's tenant-demand pipeline and infrastructure readiness. Multifamily developers active in Palm Beach Gardens should note that anchor medical facilities in mixed-use projects enhance adjacent residential absorption and pricing power; proximity to Jupiter Medical Center (and future phases) supports residential feasibility and long-term tenant retention in the submarket.

stories.title: Handshake deal could turn 17 acres of former Shell campus into Houston concert venue
stories.excerpt: The planned effort to transform a former Shell research facility into an outdoor music venue appears ready to become reality.  While the deal is not closed, a handshake deal to purchase the land has been made, according to the Houston Business Journal. Officials representing Southwest Houston’s Tax Increment Reinvestment Zone No. 20 told the outlet that the deal isn’t finalized and still needs to go before the Houston City Council, but there are agreements to purchase the 17 acres earmarked for the project.  Naina Magon, the managing principal of Hawes Hill & Associates, told the outlet that the City Council […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Handshake deal to convert 17-acre former Shell research campus to Levitt Pavilion Houston; $5.4M purchase agreed
City purchased land in 2019 for $3.4M; TIRZ-funded nonprofit music venue; Houston City Council approval pending
Placemaking/cultural amenity in southwest Houston; catalyst for adjacent residential/mixed-use opportunity
stories.summary: A handshake deal has been struck to purchase 17 acres of a former Shell research facility in southwest Houston for conversion to Levitt Pavilion Houston, a free concert venue funded by the Levitt Family Foundation nonprofit. The agreed purchase price is $5.4 million (Houston paid $3.4M in 2019). The TIRZ (Tax Increment Reinvestment Zone No. 20) is negotiating the deal, which requires Houston City Council approval in coming months. Seven Levitt Pavilions currently operate across the U.S. No designer has been selected yet.
stories.impact: Placemaking and cultural amenity development in southwest Houston (5521 Gasmer Drive) signals city commitment to spurring neighborhood revitalization and attracting foot traffic. The Levitt Pavilion will anchor local demand for dining, retail, and housing in a historically underinvested area. Multifamily developers should scout adjacent land parcels for residential projects timed to Levitt opening; the free concert venue will drive foot traffic and establish the submarket as an emerging lifestyle destination, supporting higher rents and faster leasing velocity.

stories.title: Twain Capital Supplies $65M C-PACE Loan for Colorado Hotel Project
stories.excerpt: Developer Nathaniel Kelly Foote has secured $65 million of Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy (C-PACE) debt to complete a mixed-use project with a Marriott-branded hotel in the heart of Colorado’s Rocky Mountains, Commercial Observer has learned. Twain Capital Partners provided the C-PACE loan for the 144,597-square-foot Foote’s Rest development in Frisco, Colo., that will be anchored by an 80-key Marriott Tribute Hotel. The large-scale project will feature an underground 13,500-square-foot entertainment venue with bowling lanes and golf swing suites. It is slated for completion in October 2027. Andy Meyer, managing partner of Twain, said the deal was attractive from an underwriting perspective given Colorado’s strong C-PACE program that empowers property owners to fund 100 percent of sustainability upgrades coupled with solid hospitality demand in the Frisco market. He said Frsisco, which is 26 miles east of Vail and 15 minutes north of Breckenridge Ski Resort, has a lack of hotel supply. “Frisco continues to see more year-round travel,” Meyer told CO. “It was a challenging underwrite because there are no direct comps in the Frisco market, but a hotel of this size and scale with the amenities that are proposed we believe will achieve very strong occupancy.” Located at 500 Main Street, Foote’s Rest will also include a 4,000 square-foot ballroom, a 1,500 square feet of event space, a conference center, restaurants and a 10,000 square-foot rooftop bar. Colorado-based Greenwood Hospitality will serve as operator for the property. Foote did not immediately return a request for comment. The Foote’s Rest deal adds to Twain’s strong lending volume since the St. Louis-based lender launched a C-PACE business in late 2025 to complement its bridge lending platform. Meyer said the firm has executed $265 million of C-PACE loans over the past six months and has a pipeline $140 million under contract million that it expects to close by the end of 2026. Meyer, who joined Twa
bullets: Twain Capital supplies $65M C-PACE financing for 144.6K SF Foote's Rest mixed-use project in Frisco, Colorado
80-key Marriott Tribute Hotel; underground entertainment venue (bowling, golf); completion October 2027
Mountain resort hospitality demand + C-PACE capital availability; 26 miles east of Vail; signal of supply gap
stories.summary: Twain Capital Partners provided $65 million in Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy (C-PACE) financing for Foote's Rest, a 144,597-square-foot mixed-use project in Frisco, Colorado anchored by an 80-key Marriott Tribute Hotel. The project includes an underground 13,500-SF entertainment venue with bowling lanes and golf swing suites. Frisco is 26 miles east of Vail and 15 minutes north of Breckenridge Ski Resort. Completion is slated for October 2027. Twain cited Colorado's strong C-PACE program and solid hospitality demand as underwriting drivers.
stories.impact: Strong hospitality demand in Rocky Mountain ski markets signals year-round tourism and lodging supply gaps. Frisco's proximity to Vail and Breckenridge supports demand for service-economy workforce housing (hotel staff, retail, hospitality). Multifamily developers should scout Frisco and surrounding summit-county markets for workforce apartments; hospitality-anchored mixed-use projects often drive nearby residential demand and are underpinned by stable, long-term job growth in tourism-dependent economies.

stories.title: Charles Cohen revives West Palm office project after resolving site foreclosure
stories.excerpt: Charles Cohen has set in motion a restart of his long-planned 400,000-square-foot office building in downtown West Palm Beach, after resolving a foreclosure lawsuit on a $10 million loan on the site. New York-based Cohen Brothers Realty plans the 25-story West Palm Point with ground-floor retail space and an 11-story garage with a rooftop amenity deck, according to the project’s website. The developer has started the permitting process, Palm Beach County records show.  It marks a new chapter for West Palm Point, which was first revealed in 2020, that comes after Cohen faced lawsuits alleging unpaid debts in New York […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Charles Cohen restarts West Palm Point office project after resolving $10M loan foreclosure; 400K SF, 25-story tower
Groundfloor retail, 11-story garage; permitting underway; originally proposed 2020 with 23 stories; adaptive design
WPB downtown office market strength (driven by Steve Ross) de-risks office development; Cohen repositioning after crisis
stories.summary: Charles Cohen's Cohen Brothers Realty has restarted the West Palm Point office project after resolving a $10 million foreclosure lawsuit on the development site. The 25-story, 400,000-square-foot tower (originally proposed at 23 stories in 2020) will include ground-floor retail and an 11-story garage with rooftop amenity deck. Cohen Brothers leases the 2.4-acre site from the West Palm Beach CRA. The developer has initiated the permitting process. Pre-leasing letters of intent are shown for multiple floors (4, 11–16) with two floors marked "leased" and "reserved."
stories.impact: West Palm Beach downtown office market fundamentals have strengthened since 2020 due to Steve Ross's high-profile office acquisitions and rapid repositioning; Cohen's project restart reflects improved pre-leasing confidence. However, earlier financial distress and litigation indicate development risk persists. Multifamily developers should note that WPB office market revitalization is real but still dependent on Ross momentum; secondary office projects may face headwinds if market sentiment shifts. Conversely, if West Palm office rents continue appreciating, ground-floor retail and mixed-use (office/residential) becomes more feasible.

stories.title: Nathan Berman communicating with investors over Pfizer project
stories.excerpt: While shoring up the former Pfizer headquarters in Midtown East, its developer is also looking to shore up investor support. MetroLoft’s Nathan Berman has been talking to investors of the office-to-residential project since Tuesday’s incident, the New York Times reported. Berman’s ability to meet the construction timeline relies not just on the ability to resume work at 235 East 42nd Street, but to hold on to his financing. Berman told investors that there would be relatively minor delays, but that the project would be completed next year, as intended. Berman has said he only expects the delay to last a […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: MetroLoft's Nathan Berman reassuring investors on Pfizer HQ conversion after structural incident; $700M construction loan from Madison
Structural damage (sagging floors, buckled columns) on 235 East 42nd Street; expects weeks delay, completion in 2027
NYC office-to-residential conversion financing robust; record loan size signals capital confidence despite construction risk
stories.summary: MetroLoft developer Nathan Berman has been communicating with investors following a structural incident at the former Pfizer headquarters conversion at 235 East 42nd Street. The FDNY discovered sagging upper-level floors and two buckled support columns, prompting evacuation of construction workers and nine nearby buildings. Berman characterized it as a "freak accident" involving two specific columns and expects the delay to last a few weeks, with completion still targeted for 2027. MetroLoft secured a $700 million construction loan from Madison Realty Capital in May—a record amount for a residential conversion in NY. Partners also hold $135 million in financing from Northwind Group.
stories.impact: Structural incident at high-profile Midtown East conversion highlights construction and engineering risk in large historic office-to-residential projects, but Madison Realty Capital's record $700M financing and Berman's investor confidence suggest capital markets remain bullish on NYC conversions despite setbacks. Multifamily developers considering office-to-residential plays in Manhattan should budget for structural remediation contingencies and extended timelines; however, the $700M loan size signals that conversion financing windows remain open for well-capitalized sponsors with proven execution track records.
